title: Classic high-risk ACS
patient: A 72-year-old man presents with 1 hour of crushing substernal chest pressure radiating to the left jaw, with diaphoresis and nausea. He has a prior myocardial infarction and diabetes. ECG: 2 mm ST depression in V3-V6. Troponin I 0.22 ng/mL (reference < 0.04 ng/mL; approximately 5.5 times the upper limit of normal).

title: Young atypical reproducible chest pain
patient: A 28-year-old woman reports sharp left-sided chest pain that is worse with deep breathing and reproducible with chest wall palpation, without radiation or diaphoresis. She has no known cardiac risk factors. ECG: normal sinus rhythm with no ST-segment or T-wave abnormalities. Troponin I 0.01 ng/mL (reference < 0.04 ng/mL; below the upper limit of normal).

title: Moderate-risk mixed chest pain features
patient: A 55-year-old man has pressure-like chest discomfort that began with exertion 3 hours ago but also has some pleuritic quality. He has hypertension and is a current smoker. ECG: nonspecific T-wave flattening in the lateral leads. Troponin I 0.09 ng/mL (reference < 0.04 ng/mL; approximately 2.3 times the upper limit of normal).

title: STEMI bypass to cath lab
patient: A 61-year-old man presents with 45 minutes of crushing substernal chest pain and diaphoresis. He has hypertension, diabetes, and is a current smoker. ECG: 3 mm ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF with reciprocal ST depression in I and aVL, meeting STEMI criteria. Troponin I 0.19 ng/mL (reference < 0.04 ng/mL; approximately 4.8 times the upper limit of normal).

title: Low total with markedly elevated troponin
patient: A 32-year-old woman has vague epigastric discomfort for 2 hours without radiation, diaphoresis, or an exertional component. She has no known cardiac risk factors. ECG: normal sinus rhythm with no ST-segment or T-wave changes. Troponin I 0.15 ng/mL (reference < 0.04 ng/mL; approximately 3.8 times the upper limit of normal).

title: Boundary low-risk score of three
patient: A 42-year-old man reports intermittent left-sided chest ache for 2 days that is somewhat exertional but also occurs at rest, without radiation or diaphoresis. He has hypertension. ECG: early repolarization pattern. Troponin I 0.02 ng/mL (reference < 0.04 ng/mL; below the upper limit of normal).

title: Elderly diabetic atypical ACS equivalent
patient: A 78-year-old woman presents with 2 days of fatigue and mild dyspnea on exertion without chest pain or diaphoresis. She has diabetes, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. ECG: nonspecific ST-T wave changes in the precordial leads. Troponin I 0.07 ng/mL (reference < 0.04 ng/mL; approximately 1.8 times the upper limit of normal).

title: Cocaine-associated chest pain
patient: A 34-year-old man develops substernal chest pressure 2 hours after cocaine use and is diaphoretic. His only recognized cardiac risk factor is active cocaine use. ECG: sinus tachycardia with 1 mm ST elevation in V1-V3 not meeting STEMI criteria, likely early repolarization versus cocaine effect. Troponin I 0.03 ng/mL (reference < 0.04 ng/mL; below the upper limit of normal).

title: Pending troponin prevents finalization
patient: A 52-year-old man has new exertional substernal chest pressure that resolved with rest and began 1 hour ago. He has hypertension and a family history of myocardial infarction in his father at age 50. ECG: normal sinus rhythm with no acute changes. Troponin drawn, result not yet available.

title: Pleuritic chest pain with PE concern
patient: A 25-year-old woman presents with sudden sharp right-sided chest pain that is worse with inspiration and started 6 hours ago after an 8-hour car drive. She has no known cardiac risk factors. ECG: sinus tachycardia at 102 bpm with no ST-segment or T-wave changes. Troponin I 0.01 ng/mL (reference < 0.04 ng/mL; below the upper limit of normal).

title: Known CAD with typical angina
patient: A 67-year-old man reports substernal pressure with exertion radiating to the left arm, similar to prior anginal episodes. He has prior CABG, hypertension, and diabetes. ECG: old Q waves in the inferior leads with no new acute ST-segment or T-wave changes. Troponin I 0.03 ng/mL (reference < 0.04 ng/mL; below the upper limit of normal).

title: Boundary moderate-risk score of four
patient: A 44-year-old woman has intermittent substernal tightness for 4 hours that is partially exertional, without radiation or diaphoresis. She has hyperlipidemia. ECG: nonspecific T-wave changes in leads V4-V6. Troponin I 0.05 ng/mL (reference < 0.04 ng/mL; approximately 1.3 times the upper limit of normal).

title: Hemodynamic instability bypass
patient: A 58-year-old man presents with crushing substernal chest pain, diaphoresis, altered mental status, and blood pressure 74/40 mmHg. He has hypertension, diabetes, and a prior myocardial infarction. ECG: diffuse ST-T wave changes. Troponin I 0.35 ng/mL (reference < 0.04 ng/mL; approximately 8.8 times the upper limit of normal).

title: Low score with negative serial troponins
patient: A 38-year-old man had a brief 10-minute episode of sharp left chest wall pain that was reproducible with palpation, without exertional component or radiation. He has no known cardiac risk factors. ECG: normal sinus rhythm with no abnormalities. Initial troponin I 0.01 ng/mL (reference < 0.04 ng/mL; below the upper limit of normal). Repeat troponin I at 3 hours was also 0.01 ng/mL (reference < 0.04 ng/mL; below the upper limit of normal).
